topics.title: Topic Particle 은/는
topics.summary: The topic particle 은/는 marks what a sentence is about — use 은 after a consonant and 는 after a vowel.
topics.explanation: 은/는 marks the topic, the thing the sentence is commenting on, often with a sense of contrast. Attach 은 to a noun ending in a consonant and 는 to a noun ending in a vowel. It differs from the subject particle 이/가, which simply marks the grammatical subject.

examples.translation: The book is on the desk.

exercises.explanation: 저 ends in a vowel, so the topic particle 는 is used.
동생 ends in a consonant, so 은 is used.
커피 ends in a vowel, so 는 is used.
산 ends in a consonant, so 은 is used.

topics.title: Subject Particle 이/가
topics.summary: The subject particle 이/가 marks the grammatical subject — use 이 after a consonant and 가 after a vowel.
topics.explanation: 이/가 marks the subject that performs the action or is described. Attach 이 to a noun ending in a consonant and 가 to a noun ending in a vowel. Compared with 은/는, 이/가 often introduces new or specific information.

examples.translation: The book is on the desk.

exercises.promptTranslation: A student is in the library.
exercises.explanation: 학생 ends in a consonant, so 이 is used.
바람 ends in a consonant, so 이 is used.
아이 ends in a vowel, so 가 is used.
시간 ends in a consonant, so 이 is used.

topics.title: Object Particle 을/를
topics.summary: The object particle 을/를 marks the direct object — use 을 after a consonant and 를 after a vowel.
topics.explanation: 을/를 marks the direct object, the thing the action is done to. Attach 을 to a noun ending in a consonant and 를 to a noun ending in a vowel.

exercises.explanation: 영화 ends in a vowel, so 를 is used.
밥 ends in a consonant, so 을 is used.
커피 ends in a vowel, so 를 is used.
음식 ends in a consonant, so 을 is used.

topics.title: The Copula 이다 (to be)
topics.summary: Use 이에요/예요 to say "A is B" in polite Korean.
topics.explanation: Place the copula after the noun that describes something. If the noun ends with a consonant, use 이에요; if it ends with a vowel, use 예요. This form is polite and works in everyday sentences. The ending stays attached directly to the noun.

examples.translation: Seoul is the capital of Korea.

exercises.explanation: 선생 ends with a consonant, so the polite copula is 이에요.
재미 ends with a vowel, so the polite copula is 예요.
학생 ends with a consonant, so the correct form is 이에요.

exercises.promptTranslation: Seoul is the capital of Korea ___
exercises.explanation: 수도 ends with a vowel, so the polite copula is 예요.

topics.title: Present Tense -아요/어요
topics.summary: Polite present tense adds -아요 to ㅏ/ㅗ stems, -어요 to other stems, and -해요 to 하다 verbs.
topics.explanation: To make the polite present tense, attach -아요 to verb stems whose last vowel is ㅏ or ㅗ, and -어요 to other stems. Verbs ending in 하다 become 해요. For example 가다 → 가요, 먹다 → 먹어요, 공부하다 → 공부해요.

exercises.explanation: 가다 has the vowel ㅏ, so it becomes 가요.
먹다 has no ㅏ/ㅗ, so it takes -어요 → 먹어요.
하다 verbs become 해요, so 공부하다 → 공부해요.
마시다 + -어요 contracts to 마셔요.

topics.title: Past Tense -았/었어요
topics.summary: Polite past tense in Korean is formed with -았어요, -었어요, or -했어요.
topics.explanation: To make a verb polite past, add -았어요 after a vowel, -었어요 after a consonant, and -했어요 after 하다. The choice depends on the verb stem's final sound. This form is used in everyday conversation. Remember the vowel harmony: 아 afterㅏ orㅗ, 어 after other vowels.

exercises.promptTranslation: I ___ at a cafe with a friend yesterday.
exercises.explanation: Use -았어요 after the vowel ㄱ to form the past polite.
For 하다, the past polite is -했어요.
The verb 일어나다 takes -았어요 because the stem ends with ㄴ (a consonant).
After the vowel ㅂ, use -았어요 for the past polite.

topics.title: Negation 안 and -지 않다
topics.summary: Negate a verb with 안 before it, or by attaching -지 않다 to the stem.
topics.explanation: There are two ways to negate. Put 안 directly before the verb (안 가요 = "don’t go"), or attach -지 않다 to the verb stem (가지 않아요). Both mean the same thing; 안 is more colloquial.

exercises.explanation: 안 before the verb makes it negative: 안 가요.
안 먹어요 means "doesn’t eat."

exercises.promptTranslation: My friend doesn't go to school.
exercises.explanation: The -지 않다 pattern: 가지 않아요.
오지 않아요 means "it doesn’t rain."

topics.title: Existence 있다 / 없다
topics.summary: Use 있다 and 없어요 to say something exists or does not exist.
topics.explanation: 있다 means "to exist" or "to have" and is used with the polite ending 있어요. 없어요 is the negative form meaning "does not exist" or "does not have." Place the verb after the location or object you are talking about. The sentence ends with the verb, not a particle.

examples.translation: There is a book on the desk.
There is no milk in the refrigerator.
There is a cat in our house.

exercises.promptTranslation: There is a pen on the desk.
exercises.explanation: Use 있어요 to state that the pen exists.

exercises.promptTranslation: There is milk in the refrigerator.
exercises.explanation: 없어요 is the correct negative form because the sentence says there is no milk.

exercises.promptTranslation: There is a cat in our house.
exercises.explanation: 없어요 is used to say the cat does not exist in the house.

exercises.promptTranslation: There are trees in the park.
exercises.explanation: 있다 is the plain form used to state existence of trees.

topics.title: Location Particles 에 / 에서
topics.summary: Use 에 for a destination or static location, and 에서 for the place where an action happens.
topics.explanation: 에 marks a destination (가다/오다) or where something exists (있다/없다). 에서 marks the place where an action takes place. So you go 학교에 (to school) but study 학교에서 (at school).

exercises.promptTranslation: I read a book at the library.
exercises.explanation: Reading is an action, so the place takes 에서.
가다 takes a destination, so 학교 takes 에.
Exercising is an action done at the park, so 에서.
있다 marks a static location with 에.

topics.title: Want to -고 싶다
topics.summary: Use -고 싶다 after a verb stem to say you want to do something.
topics.explanation: Take the verb stem and add 고 싶다 to express desire. In polite speech, change 싶다 to 싶어요. The construction works with any action verb. Use the appropriate verb stem before 고.

examples.translation: I want to read a book.
I want to watch a movie with my friend.
I want to study Korean tomorrow.

exercises.explanation: Use 마시고 싶어요 after 마시다 to express wanting to drink.
가고 싶어요 follows 가다 for wanting to travel.

exercises.promptTranslation: We want to wake up early tomorrow.
exercises.explanation: 고 싶어요 is the polite form after 일어나다.

exercises.promptTranslation: The children want to play in the park.
exercises.explanation: 놀고 싶어요 uses the verb 놀다 plus 고 싶어요.

topics.title: Connective -고 (and / then)
topics.summary: -고 connects two actions or clauses in sequence, meaning “and then”.
topics.explanation: Place -고 after the verb stem to link it with the next action. It shows that the second action happens after the first. The two clauses share the same subject unless otherwise marked. Use -고 in informal and formal speech alike.

examples.translation: I woke up in the morning and drank coffee.
I met a friend and watched a movie.
I finished the homework and went home.

exercises.explanation: -고 links the two actions, showing they happen sequentially.

exercises.promptTranslation: I read a book ___ listen to music.
exercises.explanation: -고 connects the verb ‘읽다’ with the following clause.
-고 is used to join the two actions in order.

exercises.promptTranslation: I opened the door ___ entered the room.
exercises.explanation: -고 links opening the door with entering the room.

topics.title: Honorifics -(으)시-
topics.summary: -(으)시- is added to verb stems to honorify the subject of the sentence.
topics.explanation: Attach -(으)시- directly after the verb stem when the subject is someone you respect. It works with all verb endings, including -아요/어요 and -습니다. The honorific does not change the meaning of the verb, only shows respect.

examples.translation: The teacher gave a presentation at the meeting.

exercises.explanation: -(으)시- is the honorific suffix to attach to 읽다.

exercises.promptTranslation: The professor explained in the lecture.
exercises.explanation: -(으)시- honors the verb 설명하다.

exercises.promptTranslation: The CEO entered the conference room.
exercises.explanation: -(으)시- is added to 들어오다 for respect.
-(으)시- honors the verb 준비하다.
